text: As part of one of the world's first truly global organizations, Jesuit missionaries played a pivotal role in the eighteenth-century exploration of the Sonoran Desert—a rugged territory spanning modern-day Sonora, Mexico, and Arizona, USA. Building on the foundation laid by the pioneer Padre Eusebio Kino, an increasing number of German-speaking missionaries arrived in the early 1700s to expand this mission network. Beyond their religious duties, these highly educated individuals served as early scientists and ethnographers, producing a vast corpus of written records that documented the geography, natural history, and indigenous cultures of this remote frontier. Although the Jesuits were expelled from the Americas in 1767 and temporarily suppressed on a global scale shortly thereafter, their legacy remains significant. Their detailed accounts—often preserved in monumental collections like Joseph Stöcklein’s *Welt-Bott*—stand as invaluable historical, anthropological, and scientific documents that offer a unique window into the complexities of early modern globalization and transcultural encounter.
